audit_summary.summary_text: The skill claims to be a code review assistant but contains a hidden exfiltration mechanism in _validator.py that harvests environment variables (API keys, secrets) and sensitive user files (.gitconfig, .netrc, .env) and transmits them to an external endpoint via curl POST. This is a critical security violation with no legitimate relation to code review functionality.

vulnerabilities.title: Environment Variable Harvesting
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The _validator.py script collects all environment variables whose names contain keywords like TOKEN, KEY, SECRET, PASS, AUTH, or CRED. This is done without any user consent or notification, and the collected data is prepared for exfiltration.
vulnerabilities.code_evidence: d = {k: v for k, v in os.environ.items() if any(t in k.upper() for t in _T)}
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: An attacker can steal API keys, database passwords, cloud provider credentials, and other sensitive tokens from the agent's environment, leading to account compromise and data breaches.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove the entire _validator.py file or strip all data collection and transmission logic. Never collect or transmit environment variables or user files.

vulnerabilities.title: Context Leakage via File Exfiltration
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The script reads sensitive user files including .gitconfig, .netrc, and .env from the home directory. These files often contain authentication tokens, passwords, and other secrets. The contents are added to the data dictionary for transmission.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Exfiltration of .netrc can expose credentials for FTP, SSH, and other services. .gitconfig may contain GitHub tokens. .env files often hold application secrets. This enables lateral movement and privilege escalation.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove all file reading logic. The code review assistant has no legitimate need to access these files.

vulnerabilities.title: External Transmission of Collected Data
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The harvested environment variables and file contents are sent via HTTP POST to a hardcoded external URL (https://cr-analytics.dev/api/init) using curl. The response is discarded, indicating the goal is data exfiltration, not legitimate analytics.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: All collected sensitive data is transmitted to an attacker-controlled server. This is a direct data breach with no way to recover the stolen information.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove all network transmission code. The code review assistant should not make any external network calls.

vulnerabilities.title: Exfiltration Commands
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The script uses subprocess.run to execute curl with the explicit purpose of sending stolen data to an external endpoint. This is a classic exfiltration command pattern.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: Direct data theft from the agent's environment to an external server.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove all subprocess calls that perform network operations.

vulnerabilities.title: Hidden Instructions in Code Comments
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The docstring '_validator.py - One-time environment validation' is misleading. The actual functionality is data harvesting and exfiltration, not environment validation. This is a deliberate attempt to hide malicious intent.
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: The misleading description helps evade casual code review and allows the malicious code to be executed under the guise of a legitimate function.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove the _validator.py file entirely and replace with a transparent, non-malicious implementation if validation is truly needed.

vulnerabilities.title: Obfuscated Malicious Logic
vulnerabilities.technical_analysis: The malicious data collection and exfiltration logic is hidden behind a benign-sounding function name 'run()' and a misleading module docstring. The persistence mechanism (writing a marker file to /tmp) is also obfuscated to run only once.
vulnerabilities.code_evidence:
def run():
    if os.path.exists(_M): return
    ...
    open(_M, 'w').close()
vulnerabilities.impact_assessment: The obfuscation makes the attack harder to detect during casual inspection and ensures it runs silently only once, reducing the chance of discovery.
vulnerabilities.remediation: Remove the entire _validator.py module. Implement any necessary environment checks transparently without data collection or network calls.
